section: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the motivation and experimental setup for studying grain size effects on Al-rich oxide film formation and corrosion resistance in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. The steel composition is Fe-30.34Mn-8.93Al-0.9C (wt%). After homogenization at 1200°C/2h and hot rolling, solution treatments at 950, 1050, and 1150°C for 2h with water quenching produced samples S950 (fine grain ~8.7 µm), S1050, S1150 (coarse grain ~32 µm), and an as-rolled reference S0. The experimental sections detail microstructural characterization (SEM/EDS, XRD 30-90°/4°min Cu Kα, EBSD, TEM, SKPFM), electrochemical tests in deaerated 3.5 wt% NaCl (OCP 3600s, EIS 10^5-0.01 Hz, potentiodynamic polarization 0.3333 mV/s, CV -1.2 to -0.3 V_SCE 5 mV/s), oxide film formation (cathodic clean -1.0 V_SCE/300s, then -0.5 V_SCE/7200s) followed by XPS, AES (3.6 nm/min, 70×70 µm), and Mott-Schottky analysis, plus 30-day immersion test. Key results from the abstract are already stated: S950 oxide thickness 25.2 nm, low defect density, corrosion rate 0.160 mm/y; S1150 oxide 18 nm, lower Al, 43% higher corrosion rate; mechanism involving grain boundary density and Al dissolution. Subsequent chunks should provide detailed EBSD grain boundary maps, electrochemical curves, XPS depth profiles, and direct structure-property correlations.
sample_aliases: S950: solution treated at 950°C, fine grain ~8.7 µm
S1050: solution treated at 1050°C, medium grain
S1150: solution treated at 1150°C, coarse grain ~32 µm
processing_route: Hot rolling at 1200–950°C to 20 mm thickness
Solution treatment at 950/1050/1150°C for 2 h + water quenching
microstructure: Austenitic matrix with XRD peaks at ~42°(111), ~48°(200), ~72°(220), ~88°(311)
properties: S950 oxide film thickness 25.2±1.2 nm, defect densities 0.68e21 and 0.26e21 cm-3
S1150 oxide film thickness 18±0.9 nm, Al content drops from 90.17 at% to 69.91 at%
mechanisms: Fine grain and high grain boundary density promote preferential Al dissolution at boundaries, forming thick, uniform Al-rich oxide film
Coarse grain leads to sparse boundaries, discontinuous oxide growth, higher Fe/Mn content, and reduced corrosion resistance
figures_or_tables: Figure 1: XRD spectra of LDSs after solution treatment
Table 1: Summary of corrosion-related literature on LDSs (Introduction)

context_update.summary: This chunk presents microstructural and electrochemical results for Fe‑Mn‑Al‑C low‑density steels (S0 as‑rolled, S950, S1050, S1150). XRD shows single‑phase FCC austenite with enhanced (111) and reduced (220) peaks after solution treatment. Grain size increases from an uneven distribution in S0 to 32 µm at 1150°C. KAM maps reveal residual stress (1.62 in S0) dropping to ~0.4 in treated condition. S950 exhibits the maximum ATBs (0.146 µm/µm²) and HAGBs (0.114 µm/µm²), while S1150 has the lowest (0.040, 0.019 µm/µm²). TEM detects κ‑carbides (5–10 nm) in S0 and reduced dislocation density plus dislocation tangles near ATBs after treatment. Electrochemical data show S0 has the highest i_bre (9.92 µA/cm²) and S950 the lowest (2.72 µA/cm²). EIS fitting gives Rp of 12486 Ω·cm² for S950 versus 6036 Ω·cm² for S0. Immersion tests yield corrosion rates: S0 0.326, S950 0.160, S1050 0.248, S1150 0.281 mm/y. All results consistently relate finer grain size to superior corrosion resistance. The next sections will use XPS/AES oxide film analysis to explain the underlying mechanism. Key figures: Fig. 1–9 and Table 2.
sample_aliases: S0 (as‑rolled, uneven grains, high residual stress, κ‑carbides)
S950 (fine grain, lowest corrosion rate, highest GB density)
S1150 (coarse grain, highest corrosion rate among treated samples)
microstructure: grain size increases with solution temperature
mechanisms: Finer grain size and higher GB density reduce corrosion rate, an effect to be linked to Al‑rich oxide film formation in subsequent sections
unresolved_terms: complete EIS fitting parameters (Rs, R1, Q1, n values) in Table 2
oxide film composition and thickness (XPS/AES data to follow)

context_update.summary: This chunk presents electrochemical and oxide film characterization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with different grain sizes. Potentiostatic polarization shows S950 has the lowest stable current density (2.45 µA/cm²). EIS after 7200 s polarization gives Rp of 21355 Ω·cm² for S950, the highest, and PE of 30.75% versus 17.95% for S1150. Mott-Schottky analysis yields defect densities: S950 has the lowest ND (0.26×10^21 cm⁻³) and NA (0.68×10^21 cm⁻³), while S0 has the highest. XPS reveals Al is predominant, with S950 achieving 90.17 at% Al in the oxide film. AES depth profiling shows oxide thickness decreases from 25.2 nm (S950) to 18 nm (S1150), with S950 film being uniform and Al-rich. These results collectively demonstrate that grain refinement promotes formation of a thick, Al-rich, low-defect oxide film conferring superior corrosion resistance. Figures 6–15 and Tables 2–3 document the evidence. Subsequent sections will likely discuss the thermodynamic and kinetic mechanisms linking grain size to oxide film quality.
processing_route: solution treatment at 950/1050/1150 °C for 2 h + water quenching
microstructure: Al-rich oxide with 90.17 at% Al on S950
properties: stable current density: S0 8.39, S950 2.45, S1050 4.52, S1150 6.04 µA/cm²
mechanisms: grain refinement promotes Al-rich oxide film
duplex semiconductor behavior with n-type and p-type regions
lower defect densities improve corrosion resistance

context_update.summary: Current chunk provides AES depth profile analysis of oxide films, reporting thicknesses: S0 ~16.2 nm, S950 ~25.2 nm, S1050 ~21.6 nm, S1150 ~18 nm. S950 exhibits a uniform, dense, thick Al-rich oxide film. The subsequent section (4.1) discusses the effect of solution temperature on microstructure, emphasizing that phase composition remains unchanged while grain size and GB density vary significantly. Grain growth is driven by free energy reduction through GB migration, described by classical D^2-D0^2 = kt kinetics with an Arrhenius-type diffusion coefficient. Higher solution temperature exponentially increases k, enhancing atomic mobility and promoting large grains swallowing small ones. HAGBs, with higher energy, strongly drive migration; ATBs, being low-energy special boundaries, are consumed at elevated temperatures. These mechanisms link solution temperature to grain size and GB density. The chunk also includes figures and tables: Fig.11 (EIS Nyquist/Bode), Table 3 (EIS fitting parameters with Rp values), Fig.12 (Mott-Schottky and defect densities), Fig.13 (XPS spectra), Fig.14 (relative content of valence states), and Fig.15 (AES depth profile). These data sets will support further discussion of oxide film formation mechanisms.
processing_route: Solution treatment at 950, 1050, 1150 °C for 2 h + water quenching
microstructure: Austenitic phase composition unchanged after solution treatment
properties: S950 oxide film thickness 25.2 nm, uniform and Al-rich
S0 oxide film thickness 16.2 nm, Fe and Al roughly equal
mechanisms: Grain growth driven by free energy minimization and GB migration
D^2 - D0^2 = kt with k = k0 exp(-Q/RT)
Higher temperature increases k and accelerates atomic mobility and grain growth
HAGBs with higher energy enhance migration; ATBs are consumed at high temperature
unresolved_terms: Activation energy Q value not given

previous_context_summary:
Summary: Subsequent sections will likely discuss the thermodynamic and kinetic mechanisms linking grain size to oxide film quality. Current chunk provides AES depth profile analysis of oxide films, reporting thicknesses: S0 ~16.2 nm, S950 ~25.2 nm, S1050 ~21.6 nm, S1150 ~18 nm. S950 exhibits a uniform, dense, thick Al-rich oxide film. The subsequent section (4.1) discusses the effect of solution temperature on microstructure, emphasizing that phase composition remains unchanged while grain size and GB density vary significantly. Grain growth is driven by free energy reduction through GB migration, described by classical D^2-D0^2 = kt kinetics with an Arrhenius-type diffusion coefficient. Higher solution temperature exponentially increases k, enhancing atomic mobility and promoting large grains swallowing small ones. HAGBs, with higher energy, strongly drive migration; ATBs, being low-energy special boundaries, are consumed at elevated temperatures. These mechanisms link solution temperature to grain size and GB density. The chunk also includes figures and tables: Fig.11 (EIS Nyquist/Bode), Table 3 (EIS fitting parameters with Rp values), Fig.12 (Mott-Schottky and defect densities), Fig.13 (XPS spectra), Fig.14 (relative content of valence states), and Fig.15 (AES depth profile). These data sets will support further discussion of oxide film formation mechanisms.
Material systems: Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel; Al-rich oxide film; austenitic matrix; Fe‑Mn‑Al‑C low‑density steel; Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels
Sample aliases: S0: as-rolled, no solution treatment; S950: solution treated at 950°C, fine grain ~8.7 µm; S1050: solution treated at 1050°C, medium grain; S1150: solution treated at 1150°C, coarse grain ~32 µm; S0 (as‑rolled, uneven grains, high residual stress, κ‑carbides); S950 (fine grain, lowest corrosion rate, highest GB density); S1050 (medium grain); S1150 (coarse grain, highest corrosion rate among treated samples)
Processing route: Vacuum melting; Homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h; Hot rolling at 1200–950°C to 20 mm thickness; Water quenching; Solution treatment at 950/1050/1150°C for 2 h + water quenching; solution treatment at 950/1050/1150 °C for 2 h + water quenching; potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl; Solution treatment at 950, 1050, 1150 °C for 2 h + water quenching
Key variables: Solution treatment temperature (950, 1050, 1150°C); Grain size (8.7–32 µm); Grain boundary density; Oxide film composition (Al, Fe, Mn); Oxide film defect density; Corrosion rate (mm/y); grain size; ATBs density
Microstructure: Austenitic matrix with XRD peaks at ~42°(111), ~48°(200), ~72°(220), ~88°(311); single‑phase FCC austenite; κ‑carbides (5–10 nm) in S0; dislocation tangles near ATBs; annealing twin boundaries; grain size increases with solution temperature; maximum HAGBs/ATBs in S950; oxide film thickness (16.2-25.2 nm)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers sections 4.1 and 4.2. Section 4.1 explains that increasing solution temperature exponentially raises the diffusion coefficient k, enhancing atomic mobility and accelerating grain boundary migration, causing large grains to swallow small grains. High-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) with higher energy drive migration more strongly, while annealing twin boundaries (ATBs), as low-energy special boundaries, are gradually consumed at higher temperatures, reducing grain boundary density and total free energy. Section 4.2 provides thermodynamic analysis for oxide film formation. Standard Gibbs free energies of formation are given for Al₂O₃, FeO, and MnO (Equations 10–12), and standard electrode potentials (Equations 13–15) confirm Al has the strongest oxidation tendency. Al preferentially forms Al³⁺, which hydrolyzes to an Al-rich Al₂O₃ protective layer that inhibits Fe and Mn oxidation. In the film, Fe exists mainly as Fe²⁺ with minor Fe₂O₃/FeOOH, while Mn has the lowest enrichment as trace Mn(OH)₂/MnO₂. E-pH diagrams (Fig. 16) confirm Al₂O₃ stability, Fe²⁺/FeO dominance, and Mn²⁺ with a close MnO₂ stability region. With higher solution temperature, the film’s Al content decreases, Fe/Mn increase, and thickness reduces. Relevant figures: Fig. 15 (AES depth profiles), Fig. 16 (E-pH diagrams), Fig. 17 (dissolution morphology and surface potential maps), and Equations 10–21.
key_variables: diffusion coefficient k (exponential increase with temperature)
standard Gibbs free energy of formation (ΔG°)
microstructure: oxide film contains Fe²⁺, FeO, trace Fe₂O₃/FeOOH, trace Mn(OH)₂/MnO₂
properties: Al strongest thermodynamic driving force for oxidation (ΔG° -756.3 kJ/mol)
Al₂O₃ stable in E-pH diagram under test conditions
mechanisms: Exponential increase of k with temperature enhances atomic mobility and grain boundary migration
Large grains swallow small grains, reducing GB density and total free energy
Al preferential oxidation forms Al₂O₃ protective layer that suppresses Fe and Mn oxidation
Fe predominantly forms Fe²⁺/FeO; Mn exists mainly as Mn²⁺ with limited oxidation

High-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) with higher energy drive migration more strongly, while annealing twin boundaries (ATBs), as low-energy special boundaries, are gradually consumed at higher temperatures, reducing grain boundary density and total free energy. Section 4.2 provides thermodynamic analysis for oxide film formation. context_update.summary: Chunk 0006 completes the thermodynamic analysis (Section 4.2) with E-pH diagrams confirming Al₂O₃ stability, FeO as main oxide, and Mn²⁺ as primary species. Section 4.3 demonstrates that corrosion resistance declines with increasing grain size, with S950 being superior. The mechanism is attributed to the higher interfacial free energy and lower dissolution barriers at grain boundaries, which promote preferential Al dissolution and rapid formation of a protective Al-rich oxide film. SEM and surface potential maps (Fig. 17) show HAGBs are most active, while ATBs assist uniform film growth. EIS immersion (Fig. 18) gives Rp of S950 = 114.9 kΩ·cm² at 168 h vs S1150 = 50.6 kΩ·cm². Cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 19) confirms Al oxidation dominates the first cycle with charge density 2.55×10⁻⁵ C/cm² for S950 and no Fe peak, indicating immediate passivation. Conclusions quantitatively summarize grain size (8.7–32 µm), HAGB density (0.114–0.019 µm/µm²), breakdown current (2.72 µA/cm²), corrosion rate 0.160 mm/y (43% lower than S1150), film defect densities (NA 0.68×10²¹, ND 0.26×10²¹ cm⁻³ for S950), and Al content drop from 90.17 to 69.91 at% with coarsening. Fig. 20 schematically contrasts small-grain and large-grain oxide film evolution.
sample_aliases: S950: fine grain (8.7 µm), 950 °C solution treated, highest corrosion resistance
S1150: coarse grain (32 µm), 1150 °C treated, lowest corrosion resistance
S0: as-rolled, no solution treatment, contains κ-carbides
processing_route: Solution treatment at 950–1150 °C for 2 h + water quenching
Electrochemical and immersion testing in 3.5 wt% NaCl
key_variables: corrosion rate (0.160 mm/y for S950, 43% lower than S1150)
Al content in oxide film (90.17→69.91 at%)
defect densities NA (0.68–higher ×10²¹ cm⁻³) and ND (0.26–higher ×10²¹ cm⁻³)
microstructure: high density of HAGBs and ATBs in fine grains
properties: lowest corrosion rate 0.160 mm/y for S950
Rp reaches 114.9 kΩ·cm² after 168 h for S950
Al-rich oxide film with 90.17 at% Al on S950, decreasing to 69.91 at% on S1150
lower defect densities in oxide film on fine-grained sample
mechanisms: GBs act as high-energy defects promoting anodic dissolution and rapid oxide nucleation
preferential Al dissolution at GBs leads to Al-rich film that suppresses Fe oxidation
ATBs aid uniform film growth via ~30 mV potential difference
coarser grains reduce nucleation sites, leading to thinner, less Al-rich, more defective film
